sentences.text: We arrived in Theresienstadt in this fortress of Empress Maria Theresa, and we were first put into barracks where men and women were separated, which was particularly hard for me because my wife was about to have a baby.
No.
My mother, my sister, and my brother-in-law were the five of us.
Our transport was named like every transport.
It was AH and my number was 712.
And I from now on became AH712.
I even lost my name and identity.
The-- again, we were under a shock.
We were torn out of our surroundings.
We were deprived of whatever earthly goods we still had-- we had a little suitcase with us-- and we were put into conditions which were absolutely inhuman in the beginning.
Such as we were in three-tiered sleeping quarters where there was no room enough.
We were under strict military rules.
We had to stand at attention as soon as an SS man came.
There was very little food and lousy food if there was any.
But in the beginning it was relatively much, much better than what later came.
And we, again, were under the shock of this transport.
And again, our heady optimism took over and after a few days, we got adjusted to it.
I got a break because I was a physician at that time, so I had a pass to go out and I could go into the female quarters and visit my wife.
And on the 9th of April, the fifth day of our internment in Theresienstadt, our boy was born.
His name was Michael.
In Czech or in Russian it is Misha, and we called him Misha.
He-- his middle initial was named after the brother who was killed during the Kristallnacht, which was Joseph.
And it was the most wonderful baby you can imagine.
The delivery was very, very difficult.
My wife had some complications which led to an enormous blood loss, and somehow they got her out of it.
And it took quite a while before she recovered from it, but she did.
Unfortunately she, she and the baby became very ill with the measles.
Measles is normally a harmless children's disease, but if it's a baby who is barely a month old, and if it is a grown up, it is a very serious illness.
And as a consequence, my wife acquired lung tuberculosis, which is a very miserable, life-threatening infection.
And it is-- at that time we didn't know the antibiotics we have now.
It's treated with lots of food and fresh air, things that were just not available in Theresienstadt.
However, we managed, all of us, to really take care of her, and it took weeks and weeks and even months until she came around and recovered and became well.
But that was much later.
In the meantime, we had to get adjusted to this new life.
My mother was a not very well lady, and she was in her 60s.
And I had to see to it that she had enough to eat, and I had my hands full.
My sister and her husband, who were also newlyweds, couldn't stand the separation.
He didn't have a pass like I did, so they volunteered to one of the transports from Theresienstadt.
They were all the time, ongoing transports to a destination which was completely unknown to us.
We only knew these transports went somewhere to the east.
Rumors had it--
That's what we were told.
They went to Poland.
Rumors had it that they went to newly organized labor camps.
And again, you know, optimism, you say, well, if you work very hard, the life is better there.
So they foolishly believed it.
I mean, sooner or later they would have been transported anyway, but they volunteered.
And I only have one sign of life from my sister, which I recovered after the war.
That was one plain postcard which was a standard saying that she was well and she needs packages of food.
And it was mailed from a little Polish town called [NON-ENGLISH], and I have no idea where she disappeared and how.
There was no trace about her husband whatsoever.
That's all I know about the fate of my only sister.
So we were in Theresienstadt, my mother, my wife, our adorable little baby, and I.
And as the time went by, we somehow got, again, adjusted to the new situation.
Can you describe, like, a typical day in Theresienstadt?
Well, I worked as a physician, and the first few weeks, as I said, my wife was in the hospital and sick.
My baby was in a children's home and, I must say, very well taken care of.
And my mother had odd jobs that the ghetto could offer.
She helped out as much as she could, and of course she was an enormous help with the baby, also.
We lived-- the men lived in barracks called the Sudeten barracks.
They were-- since it was a military garrison, there were a lot of military barracks there.
There was the Dresden barracks, which was for females.
There was a Hamburger barracks where all young women were located.
There was a Kavalir barracks, which was transformed into an old-age and a hospital for hopeless diseases.
There was another one reserved for mentally disturbed, and there was the famous [?
Hohenelbe ?] barracks where the so-called city government or Altestenrat, the-- the Council of the Elders, was having their offices.
This was all Jewish organizations.
We had a Jewish government and a Jewish administration.
And, of course, we were under strict Gestapo supervision, but they didn't dirty themselves with the Jews.
They let our people take care of the dirty work.
For instance, to put together transports to the east was a Jewish affair.
They had to determine the lists of Jews, and it was a horrible job and a lot of Altesten couldn't take it and resigned because of it.
I knew them personally.
I have a book here which was written about the one that kept a job in honor for a long time, Mr.
Edelstein, and he, in October 1944, was transported, and went to the gas chambers.
Well, we knew that they had a very tough job to do.
The medical service was headed by a Dr.
Munk with whom I didn't get along very well because he was so much of a follower of the rules.
He didn't dare, or maybe he couldn't help as much as he could have.
So I didn't have a very good relationship with him, and therefore I wound up always getting the worst jobs in the medical service.
Later on there was-- there was a German Judenalteste, and then came Mr.
Mermelstein, who was a Polish Jew.
And he was a real collaborator, I would say, and he was really miserable.
And as a matter of fact, there was a-- after liberation he was put on trial, but somehow he was not-- he was freed.
Now, when did you sense the possibility of you yourself being deported to the east?
Well, there was really no danger for us.
Number one, I was told having a baby rules us out.
Number two, that my services were essential, just the same like in [NON-ENGLISH].
And sure enough, we were in Theresienstadt for two long years.
And in these two years, believe it or not, we managed to somehow build a fairly tolerable life.
I got a hold of a manger-- it was kind of a part of a stable-- and there was a concrete feeding trough.
And I broke this down and out of this eight by 10 room, I built a little apartment for me and my wife.
You were allowed to?
The child stayed in the children's home, and we could have access to it every day.
So the rules loosened up as the time went by, but Theresienstadt was by no means a pleasant, pleasant place to be.
Compared to what followed, it was extremely pleasant.
But transport after transport poured into Theresienstadt, and mostly older people came.
They came from all over Europe.
They came from Holland.
They came from Belgium.
They came from Germany mainly, and they came from the rest of Europe, from France.
And one day there came a Viennese transport and in it was, lo and behold, the mother of my wife, my mother-in-law.
And that was a very pleasant surprise, if you can name that.
And we were extremely happy to have now both mothers with us.

Unfortunately, this happiness didn't last very long because invariably both old ladies were put into any transport that came up, but at that time I had good connections, and I always somehow could get them out of the transplant list until we reached October of 1943 when we were unable to get them out of it.
And this is one of the horrible nightmares and unforgettable experiences when you saw your beloved mother, deprived of my support and help, put into a transport.
And the last I saw was when one of these SS animals pushed her into one of these wagons which were only for, for cattle and not for people.
And that was the last I saw my mother.
Needless to say, we always were hoping against hope to somehow see them again alive, and we were talking of nothing else but the time when we will be again reunited with our mothers.
What prompted our optimism, again, we followed very closely what was going on in the world and by now, Hitler didn't do very well.
Stalin has occurred, and--
Oh, we knew everything.
We had-- There was-- there were not only SS people or Gestapo people controlling us, but there was a whole group of Czech gendarmes who were very benevolent and obviously trying to help us.
In addition, they knew that I was a doctor, and I had a good reputation.
And one of them had a tonsil abscess, which is a very painful infection in the throat, and I treated him and operated on him.
And since then, he-- he really helped us as much as he could.
And he brought us all the news.
We were informed what was going on.
We knew-- as a matter of fact, we knew much more than what's going on.
The Jewish word for fairy tales which are wishful thinking is called a [NON-ENGLISH].
And there was-- jokefully we had an agency called the [NON-ENGLISH] agency.

And these rumors, which is a [NON-ENGLISH], flew around and made our mood bearable because long before the real McCoy happened, Sicily was invaded, and Italy was invaded, and they landed in the south of France, and-- and God knows what optimistic fairy tales, and Berlin was bombed and Dresden was bombed, and this was all holidays for us when the Germans were hit.
All these [NON-ENGLISH] brought news of things that were bound to happen sooner or later.
Only in our dreams and wishes, they happened much earlier.
We were deported after the embellishment of Theresienstadt.
And I want to dwell a little on the embellishment.
Theresienstadt was a miserable place to be.
We were full of old and sick people, and there was ridiculously low rations of food, and theoretically you could not survive on the ration.
That my mother stayed alive was only because I supplied her with additional food because I had, through my profession, access to additional food.
My wife survived her tuberculosis because I could feed her properly.
But the rest of the population was in very miserable misery.
There was dysentery.
There was hunger.
There was cold.
There was depression and exhaustion.
And people died like flies.
They died in their sleep, and they died standing in line for food.
They died working.
And we had an enormous mortality rate every day.
So Theresienstadt was-- as overcrowded as it was, mother nature took care of it by an enormous mortality rate and the transport emptied it out, but new transport kept on coming in.
So finally in 1944, it somehow settled down.
All of a sudden there were no transports and once there were no transport, we-- our mothers were gone.
We were just the three of us.
And to be honest, human nature is very strange.
I remember that these few months in 1944 when we knew that the war was lost for Germany-- there was absolutely no way how he could recover from all the events that had taken place.
Now they were just fighting to stay alive.
So we knew we just had to hold on and maybe we will be able to survive it.
And then something happened.
This miserable, stinking, hungry, dysentery-plagued place overnight was built up to be a beautiful town.
They started to make a park with a gazebo in it, and in this gazebo was a music band playing music in the afternoon.
People were strolling in the park, dressed much better.
There were a street with a coffee house.
There was no coffee, but there was a coffee house.
There was a bank.
There was no money, but there was a bank with the stores, and there were little playgrounds for children.
There was a soccer field.
I mean, things that was unbelievable.
And we knew this is all phony and baloney because our life didn't [INAUDIBLE].
Rations didn't become bigger.
But we knew what it was all about because we were told there will be a movie crew to come in to film for the outside world.
What a wonderful gift Hitler gave to the Jews, that he gave them a town which is more or less like a retirement place.
By the way, the Germans that came with transports were under the-- were under the opinion that they came to a retirement place.
They were told in Germany, they were given the order to pay I don't know how many thousands of marks because it is for their retirement in Theresienstadt.
And that they will have their apartment, and they have their cleaning crew, and everything was promised to them.
For them, that was quite a shock when they saw what they came into.
So this crew of the Red Cross came from Denmark and filmed these Potemkin villages.
It was just a facade, and it took them a week to make this movie.
And we had to parade in front of the bank and the coffee house, and we had to listen to the concerts.
And then they were finished with their work.
It was quite obvious that they don't believe what they saw.
But they went and in this moment, all of Theresienstadt crumbled and was destroyed.
Because from this moment on, every day transport after transport left, and Theresienstadt was emptied out.
When did you leave?
That happened in October of 1944, and I was in the last before last of the transports on October 20th when I with my wife and my little child were put into a transport to nowhere.
We didn't know where it was going.
We--
Pardon?
What was that transport like?
That train that--
This train was a nightmare.
We were no longer in a personal car.
We were in a cattle car.
And they were-- it was meant for 40 men.
There were about 200 people in this car.
There were no facilities, no sanitary facilities.
There was no food, but we had food with us because going on a transport, we hoarded whatever we could.
So we had some food for the trip.
And the trip was endless.
And it was really bad because two of the people in our car died on the transport and had to be taken out.
And at long last, we rolled into a station through a very strange gate.
That was-- no, it was in the morning.
And the train came to a stop.
And now things happen which I-- which was very hard to believe.
Doors were opened of the rig, and in came people in striped prisoner garb.
And they were obviously Jewish and Polish, and they started to yell and scream at us and push us out of the car.
Leave everything in the car.
Whoever had a watch on his hand, they tore it off.
You don't need this anymore.
And if you have anything, give it to me because we don't need it anymore.
We were just shocked.
At that time I didn't speak any Yiddish.
I learned it only later in the camp.
But my wife did, and she tried to talk to the people, but they didn't even react.
And we were roughly pushed out of the car, and we had to line up on this ramp next to the platform, next to the train.
And we had to align ourselves in fours.
And slowly this column moved towards the end of the ramp.
Well, I saw the masses of people.
But I saw on the end of the ramp there were four immaculately uniformed SS officers.
And one of them was a stunningly beautiful man with gloves, and it was the famous and notorious Dr.
Mengele.
Well, the rumor went around, and I knew of his-- I never knew of his existence, but later on somehow I knew who he was.
And he was called the angel of death.
And this beautiful man was standing there and moved to the left or moved to the right, and you had to either go to the left or to the right.
We had no idea what it means to go to the left.
And when our turn came, here was my wife who was a young, beautiful, strong woman with a little baby on her arm.
She was moved to the left, and I was moved to the right.
I saw that they go to the left, and I tried to sneak behind to go and join them because I wanted to be with them, but I was taught the first lesson of not following orders.
I was beaten and pushed with rifle butts back to my side.
And that was the last I saw my family.
I had no idea where they went and what was in store for them.

And that was one of the [NON-ENGLISH] conditions, when they decided under technique how to eliminate a whole group of people, that it has to be done that it should not become common knowledge, that it should be secret.
And we ourselves were absolutely ignorant what was going on there.
We knew it wasn't a very nice place because the way we were treated and pushed around.
So our column was moving on and along these electrified fences of Auschwitz, and we were marched towards somewhere.
Did you know where you were?
We were-- we knew it was in Auschwitz.
Yeah, people told us.
Where are we?
We are in Auschwitz.
And we were in the part of Auschwitz called Birkenau.
Not at all.
We were-- This was the first we knew about Auschwitz-Birkenau.
Now, while we were marching, there was a guy who guarded us with a-- with a machine gun, and I tried to make conversation with him and ask him what happens to my wife and my child?
He says-- he said, and I quote, don't worry, they are taken care of.
They come into a family lager, or a family camp, and they are much better off than you are.
They will-- the women will go to work, but the children go to a children's home, and they're taken care of.
And don't worry.
Later on you will be able to see them.
That's what he told me.
And when we later on finally came to the-- to the shower or the delousing station, whatever you want to call it, we spoke to the Polish prisoners.
And they said, is that what he told you?
Don't be a fool.
See this chimney?
And he pointed at this enormous symbol of Auschwitz which was visible from everywhere, where black smoke and flames came out all the time.
He says, that's where your, your child is.
That's where your wife is.
And don't believe-- they were killed the moment you saw them part.
And when I listened to this, how could anybody with a sane mind believe such a fairy tale?

How can any nation, particularly a nation like the German one-- cultured, mother of the most unbelievable poets, musicians and scientists-- how could they be bestial enough to kill a child because he's a child?
It was just so unlikely, we just didn't want to believe it.
But the seed of this horror was planted.
And all the time there was in my mind, are they alive?
And will I see them again?
Or did they have this unbelievable, incredible fate of being killed?
But we had little time to give it any thought because we were kept busy.
We were stripped to our skin.
All our clothes were taken away, and of course, all of our meager possessions which we still had.
I happened to have a watch of my father's with me, which was a pocket watch and was very dear to me because it was-- do you want to stop now?
